one_line_takeaway: ReLoop shows that LLM-generated optimization code frequently passes the solver while being semantically wrong (a ~90pp feasibility–correctness gap), and that a ground-truth-free 'perturb-a-parameter-and-check-the-objective-moves' test plus expert-mimicking structured prompting recovers meaningful—but still far-from-complete—correctness on frontier models.
key_finding: Solver feasibility is a poor proxy for correctness: DeepSeek-V3.2 reaches 91.1% solver-feasibility on RetailOpt-190 but only 0.5% formulation correctness—a ~90-percentage-point feasibility–correctness gap. The two mechanisms are complementary by error structure: structured generation (CoT) is the dominant accuracy driver on compositional problems (+8.5pp strict accuracy on RetailOpt-190 for Claude, 22.6%→31.1%), while L2 behavioral verification dominates on localized defects (+4.4pp on MAMO-ComplexLP for Claude, its largest single L2 gain). Full ReLoop reaches 100% executable code on Claude for RetailOpt-190, but strict accuracy plateaus at 31.1% (two-thirds remain silent failures), because the residual errors are structural (internally-consistent but wrong decompositions) that perturbation cannot detect.
core_contribution: ReLoop is a training-free pipeline that improves the RELIABILITY (not security) of LLM-generated optimization code by attacking 'silent failures'—code that executes and returns a solver-feasible solution yet encodes a semantically wrong formulation. The paper's own two-part contribution is: (1) Structured generation, a single-call four-stage chain-of-thought (understand → formalize → synthesize → verify) that mirrors expert OR-modeling practice, including explicit variable-type reasoning (continuous/integer/binary) and a self-completeness check; and (2) Behavioral verification, which the authors claim is the first method to detect silent failures WITHOUT ground truth. It operationalizes a 'perturbation sensitivity' property: for a correct model, perturbing a parameter that governs a binding constraint or a non-negligible objective term must change the optimum substantially; if an extreme perturbation (e.g., capacity ×0.001, cost ×0.001, revenue/demand ×100) produces near-zero objective change, the corresponding constraint/term is likely missing. This is wrapped in a two-layer verifier (L1 execution/solver recovery with IIS + unbounded-ray diagnostics; L2 Constraint-Presence-Testing and Objective-Presence-Testing) plus diagnosis-guided, regression-guarded repair, and a released benchmark, RetailOpt-190. NOTE: the abstract snippet in the record ('Safety check: blocks data-variable reassignment...') is NOT a security mechanism—it is a repair guardrail that stops the repair LLM from fabricating data values that corrupt the problem instance.
threat_model: Not applicable—this is not a security paper and defines no adversary. The 'failure' being mitigated is an unintentional model-correctness error made by an LLM translating a natural-language optimization problem into solver code (a feasibility–correctness gap), not a malicious input, poisoning, jailbreak, or extraction attack. The only adversarial-sounding component, the repair 'safety check' that blocks data-variable reassignment and dangerous imports, guards against the repair LLM accidentally fabricating/overwriting problem data during self-repair, not against an attacker. No threat model should be forced onto this paper.
limitations: 1) The headline reliability gain is modest in absolute terms: even with full ReLoop, Claude reaches only 31.1% strict / 35.3% practical accuracy on RetailOpt-190, so ~two-thirds of feasible outputs are still silently wrong. 2) Structured generation assumes format compatibility—CoT breaks narrowly-tuned SFT models: OptMATH's MAMO Base accuracy collapses from 56.2% to 30.0% under CoT (84 crashes, 65 previously-correct solutions destroyed), and DeepSeek's RetailOpt execution collapses 91.1%→53.2% before L1 recovers it. 3) L2 reuses the same generating LLM to extract candidate constraints/objective terms, creating potential failure correlation (a missed constraint may never be tested). 4) Perturbation testing detects only localized omissions; it is blind to structural decomposition errors, coefficient-magnitude errors, and formulation-equivalence errors (explicitly listed as out of scope). On IndustryOR the correctable band is nearly empty (34% of deviations <1% subtle, 47% >10% structural). 5) The high OptMATH Base score may reflect train/eval overlap. 6) ReLoop adds ~3× base LLM token cost. 7) 32B-scale models (Qwen3, OptMATH, SIRL) achieve near-zero accuracy on RetailOpt-190 regardless of ReLoop, so gains are concentrated in frontier models.

datasets_used: RetailOpt-190 (released by the authors): 190 multi-period retail inventory optimization instances = 38 archetypes × 5 numerical variants, across 8 scenario families (F1–F8); 3–5 products over 4–8 periods, 20+ parameters, 10–30 jointly-binding constraints; ground truth = hand-crafted modular MILP solved with Gurobi 11.0
MAMO-ComplexLP: 203 instances (challenging subset of MAMO), shorter-prompt LP/MILP with well-isolated constraints (~459 tokens avg)
IndustryOR: 100 real-world instances requiring complex multi-step reasoning
quantitative_results: Feasibility–correctness gap: DeepSeek-V3.2 = 91.1% solver-feasibility vs 0.5% strict correctness on RetailOpt-190 (~90pp gap)
RetailOpt-190 Claude Opus 4.6 Exec%: Base 72.1 → CoT 93.7 → ReLoop 100.0
RetailOpt-190 Claude Acc%(ε=1e-4): Base 22.6 → CoT 31.1 → ReLoop 31.1 (strict plateau); Acc%(ε=1e-2): 26.8 → 34.7 → 35.3
RetailOpt-190 DeepSeek-V3.2 Exec%: 91.1 → 53.2 (CoT collapse) → 97.4 (L1 recovery); Acc%(1e-4): 0.5 → 3.7 → 5.8; Acc%(1e-2): 3.7 → 5.8 → 11.1
RetailOpt-190 weak 32B models near-zero accuracy; ReLoop still lifts execution (OptMATH-32B Exec 2.6 → 17.9; SIRL-32B Exec 0.0 → 1.6)
MAMO-ComplexLP Acc%(ε=1e-6): Claude 70.4→73.9→79.8; DeepSeek 60.1→59.6→62.6; Qwen3-32B 40.4→37.4→46.3; OptMATH 56.2→30.0→31.0 (SFT collapse under CoT); SIRL 53.2→46.8→54.2

Ablation attribution: structured generation (CoT) = +8.5pp strict acc on Claude RetailOpt (20 corrected, 4 regressed); L1 = +44.2pp Exec for DeepSeek RetailOpt and +5.8pp for Claude; L2 = +4.4pp on Claude MAMO (11 corrected, 2 regressed), +2.0pp DeepSeek MAMO; DeepSeek +12.0pp on IndustryOR mainly via L1
OptMATH CoT failure detail: 84 instances crash, 65 previously-correct solutions destroyed

Thresholds: L2 WARNING r<τℓ=5%, INFO 5%–30%, PASS >τh=30%; repair regression guard τr=4%; accuracy varies <1% across τℓ∈[1%,10%], τh∈[20%,50%]; ReLoop ≈ 3× base token cost; substitution-direction reversal ~35% error rate across four frontier LLMs (pilot)
baselines_compared: Ablation configurations: Base/Direct generation, CoT (structured generation only), +CoT+L1, full ReLoop (+CoT+L1+L2 with repair, N=3)
Models benchmarked: Claude Opus 4.6, DeepSeek-V3.2 (671B MoE), Qwen3-32B, OptMATH-Qwen2.5-32B (offline SFT), SIRL-Qwen2.5-32B (online RL)

Conceptually contrasted verification paradigms (Table 1, not re-run): solver feedback, OptiChat, Self-Refine, Reflexion, LEVER (LEVER needs ground-truth labels); none detect silent failures without ground truth per the paper's positioning
